exercises.title: Trace HashSet uniqueness
hints: Duplicates are silently ignored: apple+apple → 1 apple
3 unique elements: apple, banana, cherry
contains is O(1)

exercises.title: Deduplicate a list
exercises.instructions: Fill in the blank to remove duplicates from the list using a HashSet.
hints: new HashSet<>(collection) adds all, deduplicating automatically
Aisha: 1, Raj: 1, Priya: 1 → 3 unique

exercises.title: Basic Set operations
exercises.scenario: Build a permission set and check membership.
exercises.instructions:
Create a `Set<String>` with permissions: READ, WRITE, DELETE.
Check if WRITE is allowed and if EXECUTE is allowed.

Expected output:
WRITE allowed: true
EXECUTE allowed: false
Total permissions: 3

test_cases.expected_stdout:
WRITE allowed: true
EXECUTE allowed: false
Total permissions: 3

hints: Set<String> with add()
contains() for membership check — O(1)
size() for total

exercises.title: Deduplicate and count unique items
exercises.scenario: Count unique elements from a data source.
exercises.instructions:
Given an array of city names with duplicates, count how many UNIQUE cities there are.

Input: [Hyderabad, Mumbai, Hyderabad, Chennai, Mumbai, Bengaluru, Delhi]

Expected output:
Unique cities: 5
hints: Add all cities to HashSet — duplicates auto-ignored
unique.size() gives count of unique cities
Or: new HashSet<>(Arrays.asList(cities)).size()

exercises.title: Find duplicate elements
exercises.scenario: Use Set to identify which elements appear more than once.
exercises.instructions:
Given an int array, find all elements that appear MORE THAN ONCE. Print them in sorted order.

Input: [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6, 5, 3]

Expected output:
Duplicates: [1, 3, 5]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Find duplicates using two Sets
hints: seen.add(n) returns false if n was already in the set
If add returns false → duplicate found
TreeSet keeps duplicates sorted automatically
1, 3, 5 each appear twice

exercises.title: Set intersection — common skills
exercises.scenario: Find elements present in both sets.
exercises.instructions:
Two sets of programming languages known by each person. Find languages BOTH know (intersection).

Aisha: [java, python, sql]
Raj: [python, javascript, java]

Expected output:
Common: [java, python]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Set intersection to find common languages
hints: Copy first: new HashSet<>(aisha) — don't mutate original
retainAll(raj) keeps only elements in both sets
Sort for deterministic output

exercises.title: All three set operations
exercises.instructions:
Two sets of student IDs in different courses. Compute all three operations.

Course A: {S01, S02, S03, S04}
Course B: {S03, S04, S05, S06}

Expected output:
Union (all enrolled): [S01, S02, S03, S04, S05, S06]
Intersection (both courses): [S03, S04]
Only in A: [S01, S02]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// All three set operations: union, intersection, difference

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Union (all enrolled): [S01, S02, S03, S04, S05, S06]
Intersection (both courses): [S03, S04]
Only in A: [S01, S02]

hints: Always COPY before mutating: new HashSet<>(original)
Union: addAll(other)
Intersection: retainAll(other)
Difference A-B: removeAll(other)

exercises.title: Visited URL tracker (web crawler pattern)
exercises.scenario: The 'seen' pattern for processing unique items.
exercises.instructions:
Simulate processing URLs where some appear multiple times. Use a Set to process each URL EXACTLY ONCE.

URLs to process: [/home, /about, /home, /products, /about, /contact, /products]

Expected output:
Processing: /home
Processing: /about
Processing: /products
Processing: /contact
Total processed: 4
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// URL processor — process each URL exactly once

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Processing: /home
Processing: /about
Processing: /products
Processing: /contact
Total processed: 4

hints: visited.add(url) returns TRUE if newly added, FALSE if already present
Only process when add() returns true
This is the web crawler pattern!

exercises.title: Permission checker with containsAll
exercises.scenario: Does user have all required permissions?
exercises.instructions:
Check if a user has ALL required permissions using Set.containsAll().

Test 3 users:
- Aisha: [READ, WRITE, DELETE, ADMIN]
- Raj: [READ, WRITE]
- Priya: [READ, WRITE, DELETE]

Required: [READ, WRITE, DELETE]

Expected output:
Aisha authorized: true
Raj authorized: false
Priya authorized: true
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Permission check using containsAll

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Aisha authorized: true
Raj authorized: false
Priya authorized: true

hints: containsAll(required) returns true if user has ALL required permissions
Raj only has READ+WRITE, missing DELETE → false
Aisha has DELETE+more → true; Priya has exactly the required three → true

exercises.title: First non-repeated character
exercises.scenario: Set to find characters appearing exactly once.
exercises.instructions:
Find the first character in a string that appears exactly ONCE.

Input: 'aabbcddefa'

Expected output:
First unique: c
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Use Map for counts, then find first char with count==1
hints: Count all characters first with Map<Character, Integer>
Then scan string again — first char where count == 1
Scan the original string (not map) to preserve order
a:2, b:2, c:1, d:2, e:1, f:1 → first with count 1 is 'c'

exercises.title: Trace set operations — don't mutate originals
hints: copy = new HashSet<>(a) — copy made, a unchanged
copy.retainAll(b) mutates COPY, not a
a still has 4 elements
copy intersection = {3, 4}

exercises.title: User session tracker with HashSet
exercises.scenario: Track active users, block suspicious ones.
exercises.instructions:
Build a `SessionTracker`:
- `Set<String>` activeUsers (logged in)
- `Set<String>` blockedUsers
- `login(String userId)` — adds to active if not blocked
- `logout(String userId)` — removes from active
- `block(String userId)` — adds to blocked, removes from active
- `isActive(String id)` and `isBlocked(String id)` checks

Expected output:
Aisha active: true
Raj blocked: true
Raj active: false
Active users: 2
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// SessionTracker with two Sets

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Aisha active: true
Raj blocked: true
Raj active: false
Active users: 2

hints: Two Sets: active and blocked
login: check blocked first; if blocked, return false
block: add to blocked, remove from active
isActive/isBlocked: contains() — O(1)

exercises.title: Product recommendation engine
exercises.instructions:
Build a simple recommendation engine:
- User has a Set<String> of viewed products
- Catalog is a Set<String> of all products
- `recommend(Set<String> viewed, Set<String> catalog)` returns all catalog products NOT YET viewed (set difference)

Test: viewed=[Mouse, Keyboard], catalog=[Mouse, Keyboard, Monitor, Headphones, USB Hub]

Expected output:
Recommendations: [Headphones, Monitor, USB Hub]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Recommendation via Set difference: catalog minus viewed
hints: Copy catalog: new HashSet<>(catalog)
removeAll(viewed) removes already-seen products
Remaining = unseen = recommendations
Sort for deterministic output

exercises.title: Social network — mutual friends
exercises.instructions:
Build a `SocialGraph` where each user has a `Set<String>` of friends.
- `addFriendship(a, b)` — mutual (adds both directions)
- `mutualFriends(a, b)` — intersection of friend sets
- `suggestions(user)` — friends-of-friends who aren't already friends (exclude self)

Test graph: Aisha↔Raj, Aisha↔Priya, Raj↔Vikram, Priya↔Vikram

Expected output:
Aisha & Raj mutual: [Priya]
Suggestions for Aisha: [Vikram]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// SocialGraph with Set-based friend lists

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Aisha & Raj mutual: [Priya]
Suggestions for Aisha: [Vikram]

hints: Map<String, Set<String>> — each user maps to their friend set
mutual: intersection of two friend sets
suggestions: union of friends' friend-sets, minus self and existing friends
Aisha's friends: Raj, Priya. Raj's friends include Vikram. Priya's friends include Vikram. suggestions = {Vikram} - {Raj, Priya, Aisha} = {Vikram}

exercises.title: Course enrollment validator
exercises.scenario: Set-based prerequisite checking in a university system.
exercises.instructions:
Build an enrollment validator:
- `Course`: code, title, `Set<String>` prerequisites
- `Student`: name, `Set<String>` completedCourses
- `Validator`: `canEnroll(student, course)` — true if student has completed ALL prerequisites
- `missingPrerequisites(student, course)` — returns Set<String> of missing prereqs
- `eligibleCourses(student, List<Course>)` — returns courses student CAN enroll in

Expected output:
Aisha can enroll in DSA: true
Raj can enroll in DSA: false
Raj missing: [Java101]
Aisha eligible courses: [DSA, WebDev]
exercises.starter_code:
import java.util.*;

// Course enrollment validator with Set-based prerequisite checking

test_cases.expected_stdout:
Aisha can enroll in DSA: true
Raj can enroll in DSA: false
Raj missing: [Java101]
Aisha eligible courses: [DSA, WebDev]

hints: canEnroll: completed.containsAll(prerequisites) — subset check
missing: copy prerequisites, removeAll(completed) — difference
eligibleCourses: filter by canEnroll for each course
Aisha has Java101+Math101 → can do DSA and WebDev, but not ML (needs Python101)
